segments.text: Software development is changing at a
very very rapid pace. So today I want to
take a few minutes to take you through
some ideas, some techniques, give you
some tricks on how you can improve your
work as a software developer and how you
can keep up with AI. First, let me tell
you the way I see AI helping developers.
There are two groups. The first group is
when you use AI as a copilot. And most
of the people that I've been working
with, most of the people that I've met,
this is what they're doing where they
are driving, they are writing most of
the code, they are leading and then
they're using AI to help with their
work. They use AI as a copilot. Now more
and more right now that we have tools
like Codec, OpenAI Codec or Anthropic
Claude Code, more and more people,
developers, are transitioning to serve as
the copilot to AI where they are letting
AI drive. They're letting AI take the
lead and they are stepping back and
helping AI do the job. And these tricks
here, this mental shift is what I want
to cover today. Going from using AI as a
copilot to an AI-first development
workflow requires a fundamental mental
shift. You cannot just expect this to
just happen. You have to acquire the
skills that are going to help you make
this transition. Now, in order to do
that, I'm going to propose if you're
studying now a seven-step workflow. This
is the workflow that I use when building
together with AI, when letting AI lead.
Planning is the first step here. You
need to know what you're building before
you start. And before you even think
about AI, you have to understand the
area, the moment, the time where AI will
help you and where AI might not be that
useful. So for example, whenever you're
creating boilerplate or scaffolding,
AI is great for that. Obviously,
repetitive transformations when you're
creating or when you're building a
prototype, when you're generating test
cases, creating and reviewing
documentation,
refactoring, all of those are
opportunities for you to use AI to help
you. However, there are many many times
where AI might not be that helpful. If
you're working with logic that's not
properly documented or is too complex,
when you're working with security
critical code, when you're doing
performance optimizations, when you're
debugging bugs that are very subtle, very
hard to find, AI might not work as well.
Now assuming that you have gotten into
an area where you're actually you can
actually use AI. I want you to start
shifting from imperative prompting to
declarative prompting. So here is the
idea. So far people have been using AI
by asking models exactly what they need
to do. Basically you instruct the model,
hey create a page, add these components
to that page, make it work this way,
and I want you to start transitioning
to telling AI what you want to
accomplish, what your goals are.
That is declarative prompting. This is
an incredibly important shift especially
now with the current generation of
models. The latest models from
Anthropic, the latest set of open-source
models, models like Mini Max, the
latest version of DeepSeek Kimmy. These
models are every day more capable of
taking our requests and interpreting
those requests and creating their own
solutions. I don't want you to be that
solution-oriented. Instead, focus more on
goals. So, another trick here for you to
start combining exploratory sessions
with upfront planning. Upfront planning
is still very very important. If you
want to unlock longer sessions with AI
where you sit down, you give a bunch of
instructions and you can just leave and
let AI do stuff. Upfront planning is
very important. You're going to have to
provide a lot of requirements. But that
is not the only way to write code. You
should combine upfront planning with
exploratory sessions where you do not
give that many details. Instead, you let
AI figure things out. And the reason
this is so important, AI will start
surprising you. AI will start finding
solutions that maybe you didn't think
about. So it is a good idea to have for
example two sessions where you're doing
something here that you know exactly how
it needs to happen. So you did some
upfront planning and something here
where you're exploring and you can throw
that away if it doesn't work. You can
use GitHub Worktrees for example. So
if it doesn't work you throw it away but
AI will start surprising you. Upfront
planning requires you to frontload your
thinking. You're going to be putting
effort into specking out everything
that you need AI to do. The idea here
is for you to break down the problem,
define what done looks like, exactly
what you want to accomplish, what your
goals are, and you're going to try to
document constraints and edge cases to
show AI what it needs to do and the
things that it should not be doing.
The more clarity and context you provide
up front, the better the agent will be
able to execute that autonomously. The
autonomous word here is super important
because that's where leverage exists.
Whenever you can just basically give
something to the agent and unlock a very
long session of coding. You don't want
to constantly be going back and forth,
that's going to slow you down. So the
more you can provide up front, the
better chance you have for the agent to
have a long session. You want to reduce
the back and forth. Here is a tip for
those of you who use Claude Code. And
these tips even though I'm focusing on
Claude Code because that's what I'm
using. Most of these tips are going to
work with Cursor with the agent from
Cursor or with Codec. Ask Claude Code
to specify in your CLAUDE.md before
writing any code, describe the approach
and wait for approval. So if you are the
type of person who really likes to
ensure you're doing enough upfront
planning, make sure Claude does not write
a lot of code without asking for your
approval. After planning we get into
breaking things down. Very very
important, very related with planning. I
want you to start breaking the work you
give to these agents into single
responsibility tasks. Basically, one
trick. If whatever you're telling the
agent contains the word "and," you're
probably asking for too much. So, break
that down. If you find your agent
struggling, probably your scope is too
big. You need to ask the agent to break
down whatever request you gave it into
individual features or into individual
smaller tasks that the agent can tackle
in a better way. From my experience, the
more contained these tasks are, the
smaller they are, the more focused they
are, the better quality is going to come
out of this model, the better chance
you're going to have of getting good
results. Here is another tip for those
using Claude Code, write down in
CLAUDE.md if a task requires changes in
more than three files, break it down
into smaller tasks first. So it's kind
of like giving it a clue that you don't
want to make changes that affect too
much in your code. Instead, you want to
get smaller changes one at a time. This
is going to help Claude realize that this
might be a big change. So before making
it, I actually need to break it down into
smaller tasks.
Another idea is to create a decompose
command. If you're using Claude Code, you
can create skills, you can create
commands as well, slash commands. So
create a decompose command where you can
just provide your plan. Very very simple
for you to do this quickly. The
description of the command is going to
tell Claude to break things down. Break
it into single responsibility tasks. So
you can quickly say decompose and this
is the feature that I want to build and
Claude will give you back a plan composed
of multiple single responsibility tasks
which is a great, faster way of
accomplishing this. Area number three
of our plan is to be specific. It's just
like having somebody working for you.
The more specific you are, the better
results you're going to get. The more
vague you are, the harder it is going to
be for agentic tools to provide the
results that you're looking for. So,
here is the anatomy or the way I think
about a good plan. How do I create a plan
that my agentic tool understands? Okay,
so I think about these four areas. You
want to specify the "what" you want to
accomplish. So for example, hey write a
function that receives two numbers and
outputs the result of this certain
formula. Now you need the "how" of the

the approach you want the agent to
follow. Unless this is an exploratory

how exactly you want the agent to
accomplish something. So for example,
hey I want you to use recursion to solve

"where" and basically this will give the

files you should never touch. And
finally you want the "not." And the "not"
is one of the most important things in a
plan. You want to specify the areas, the
things that are adjacent to your plan
that you want your agent to ignore. Do
not implement this. I do not care about
doing that. That will give a lot of
information to your agent. So, anytime
you're thinking about a plan and you
want to be very specific, think about
these four areas and make sure your plan
contains all four of them. Another good

When you're trying to ask the agent to

what good looks like. Give the agent
examples of the format that you're
expecting back, of the boundaries on
dependencies. Give specific examples so
the agent knows and has something to look
at in order to produce a better result.
Ask the agent to explain its approach.
Something that happens is that these
agents are going to make assumptions
because it doesn't matter how much time
you spend creating a plan. It's usually
very hard to specify everything. So
there's going to be some assumptions,
just like people. These agents are going
to make assumptions about things that
you might need and how you might want

assumptions. Some of those assumptions

Sometimes these assumptions are going to

something good or something that you

this early on. A tip for Claude Code,
always reference files directly. I've
seen many people using Claude Code and

to happen. Try to specify files

sponsors that are in whatever section of

different code depending on what your

depending on the context. So, make sure
you always provide assumptions
beforehand or ask the agent to list any

create better context for your
agent. A tip for Claude Code: Describe

CLAUDE.md file. In fact, if you ask

I've seen to your CLAUDE.md file. And the

Claude does not have to do that thinking

your usage of Claude Code. And one of the

for some reason, security files, stuff

Claude will never read or modify those

number five. Remember there are seven

mercilessly. Agents make mistakes.
Period. Doesn't matter how much effort

review. Remember that AI generated code

might cause problems in other areas of

Focus on changes that are unrelated to
the code and the comments that you're

because it's very very simple to get

that helps me a lot, and this is a very

I want to make sure certain

I'm just going to write a test case and

Claude Code to find the proper solution

that way I get way stronger solutions
by forcing Claude to do this.
Write on your CLAUDE.md: "When there is a

Very good trick to make sure that Claude

I do this and I can just call that

And the point here of working with

Then we're giving the agent feedback

Agents like to over-complicate things.
Many many times I've asked my agent to

So something that I do is I have several

this is not for the faint of heart.
Don't be afraid of running Claude by
